coredata.dc:title: Structural study of B-N Co-doped carbon dots: Comparison of spectroscopic analysis using DFT
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Navarro-Ibarra, D.C.
Ibarra-Prieto, H.D.
Garcia-Garcia, A.
Aguilera-Granja, F.
coredata.dc:description: Understanding the internal geometric structure of boron–nitrogen (B–N) Co-doped carbon dots (CDs) is crucial for tailoring their properties to meet the requirements of advanced applications in bioimaging, sensing, and photovoltaics. In this study, a comprehensive Density Functional Theory (DFT) analysis was conducted to investigate the formation and arrangement of individual layers within B–N Co-doped CDs—an area that remains insufficiently explored. Thirteen distinct putative ground-state geometries were identified using the B3LYP/6-31G* level of theory. These structures were selected based on experimental insights into the material’s chemistry and correspond to low-energy local minima. While they may not represent the absolute minimum configuration, they are physically plausible and relevant for modeling the diversity of bonding environments within B–N Co-doped CDs. Comparison between theoretical and experimental FTIR and Raman spectra revealed that no single structure fully accounts for the observed spectral features. However, a composite model—constructed by combining spectra from several low-energy configurations with assumed equal statistical weight—showed significantly improved agreement with experimental data. These results suggest that B–N Co-doped CDs exhibit a heterogeneous internal architecture composed of a mixture of closely related molecular structures. This work provides new insights into the structural complexity of doped carbon dots. It offers a solid theoretical basis for guiding future efforts to optimize their electronic and optical properties for a broad range of technological applications.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Theoretical FTIR and Raman spectra of B-N Co-doped carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic representation of a doped carbon dot (CD) featuring an internal structure composed of multiple graphene layers. Each layer exhibits distinct doping and functionalization configurations, incorporating atoms such as nitrogen, boron, and oxygen. The layers are depicted in a stratified arrangement, highlighting their structural organization and chemical modifications.
Optimized atomic configurations of B-N Co-doped CDs with binding energy respectively in each case.
XPS Survey spectrum of the B-N Co-doped CDs.

Calculated FTIR spectra for 13B-N Co-doped CDs structures, arranged by stability. The experimental spectrum is shown at the bottom (500–4000 cm−1), with color bars indicating vibrational modes: light orange for boronic acid, light blue for amine, and light green for C–C aromatic interactions.

Optimized atomic configurations of a bilayer with the carbon rings shifted. In (a) side view of a bilayer, one sheet has one nitrogen atom and the other boron atom. In (b) is a top view of the bilayer. In (c) a side view of an optimized atomic configuration of a bilayer (rotated) with boronic acid, and also with amine group is presented, and in (d) is a top view of the bilayer. Finally, the energy difference and force between plates of the bilayer are graphed in (e) and (f), respectively.

The weighted average FTIR spectrum was calculated by assuming equal probability contributions from all structures. In (a), (b), and (c) is the mixture of the 3, 6, and 10 more stable configurations of B-N Co-doped CDs, respectively. In (d), the mix of all the structural arrays for B-N Co-doped CDs is presented. Also, color bars are included to identify the vibration modes presented in Table 1, and as an inset, the image of the groups contributing to the vibration of some relevant vibrational modes.
Calculated Raman spectra for the 13 structural arrays for the B-N Co-doped CDs, in order by stability. At the bottom, the Raman experimental spectrum is presented in the range of 500 to 4000 cm−1.

The weighted average Raman spectrum was calculated by assuming equal probability contributions from all structures. In (a), (b), and (c) is the mixture of the 3, 6, and 10 more stable configurations of B-N Co-doped CDs, respectively. In (d) the mix of all the structural arrays for B-N Co-doped CDs is presented. Finally, in (d) the experimental Raman spectra, are in the range of 0 to 4000 cm−1.

The weighted average Raman spectrum was calculated by assuming equal probability contributions from all structures. In (a), (b), and (c) is the mixture of the 3, 6, and 10 more stable configurations of B-N Co-doped CDs, respectively. In (d) the mix of all the structural arrays for B-N Co-doped CDs is presented. Finally, in the bottom (d) the deconvolution of the experimental Raman spectra, in the 1000 to 2000 cm−1 range. Color bars are included to show the deconvolution band of the Raman spectra.
FTIR vibrational modes and XPS assignments.

head.ce:title: Structural study of B-N Co-doped carbon dots: Comparison of spectroscopic analysis using DFT

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding the internal geometric structure of boron–nitrogen (B–N) Co-doped carbon dots (CDs) is crucial for tailoring their properties to meet the requirements of advanced applications in bioimaging, sensing, and photovoltaics. In this study, a comprehensive Density Functional Theory (DFT) analysis was conducted to investigate the formation and arrangement of individual layers within B–N Co-doped CDs—an area that remains insufficiently explored. Thirteen distinct putative ground-state geometries were identified using the B3LYP/6-31G* level of theory. These structures were selected based on experimental insights into the material’s chemistry and correspond to low-energy local minima. While they may not represent the absolute minimum configuration, they are physically plausible and relevant for modeling the diversity of bonding environments within B–N Co-doped CDs. Comparison between theoretical and experimental FTIR and Raman spectra revealed that no single structure fully accounts for the observed spectral features. However, a composite model—constructed by combining spectra from several low-energy configurations with assumed equal statistical weight—showed significantly improved agreement with experimental data. These results suggest that B–N Co-doped CDs exhibit a heterogeneous internal architecture composed of a mixture of closely related molecular structures. This work provides new insights into the structural complexity of doped carbon dots. It offers a solid theoretical basis for guiding future efforts to optimize their electronic and optical properties for a broad range of technological applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) have rapidly gained prominence in nanomaterials research due to their tunable optical properties, biocompatibility, and eco-friendly synthesis methods. Various synthesis techniques, including hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, and electrochemical processes, have been developed to control CDs' size, shape, and surface functionalization. These advancements enable the optimization of their quantum yield and photoluminescence properties [1–5].

A significant advancement in CDs research involves doping these nanomaterials with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, and boron. Doping modifies the electronic structure of CDs, enhancing their performance in various applications, including bioimaging, drug delivery, photocatalysis, environmental sensing, photovoltaics, and LED technologies [1,2,3,6–12]. For instance, nitrogen-doped CDs exhibit improved fluorescence and electron transfer capabilities by introducing new energy states that facilitate charge carrier dynamics [1,3,8,11]. Similarly, boron and sulfur dopants enhance the stability and conductivity of CDs, broadening their applicability inenvironmental sensing and energy conversion [1,3,7,13]. Moreover, doped B-N Co-doped CDs have shown significant promise in drug delivery applications, where their enhanced fluorescence and functionalization facilitate targeted therapy and controlled release mechanisms [9,10].

Despite these advancements, the theoretical understanding of CDs, particularly regarding their internal geometric structures, remains limited. Experimental studies have predominantly focused on surface functionalization, with fewer investigations addressing the core structural arrangements that significantly influence CDs' electronic and optical properties.

The electronic properties and quantum confinement of Carbon Dots (CDs) are closely tied to their carbon core structure and specific features, including defects and edge terminations[4]. This connection shows how the natural morphology of a CD and its surface modifications influence its optical performance. Density Functional Theory (DFT) is a valuable tool for researchers, enabling them to model complex atomic systems. This technique offers insights into how doping and surface modifications impact key properties, including bandgap, charge distribution, and quantum confinement effects [1,6,11,14–16].

DFT studies on nitrogen-doped CDs, for example, have demonstrated that doping can reduce the bandgap, enhance charge separation, and improve quantum efficiency [1,11,14]. These computational insights guide experimental efforts to optimize CDs performance for specific applications. However, DFT also has limitations, particularly concerning computational costs for large systems and the accurate modeling of long-range interactions, such as Van der Waals forces [6,11]. Additionally, while DFT is effective for ground-state properties, it requires more sophisticated methods to accurately capture the excited-state dynamics involved in processes such as photoluminescence [11].

Addressing the internal structure of CDs is crucial, as core defects and heteroatom incorporation can lead to localized charge distributions and enhanced electronic interactions, which are essential for optimizing performance in optoelectronic and catalytic applications [7,11,13,16]. Furthermore, as critically reviewed by Dua et al. [13], the stability of B-N Co-doped CDsis intrinsically linked to their internal structural integrity, which impacts their long-term performance in commercial and application-specific contexts. Understanding these internal geometric configurations offers a pathway to fine-tuning CDs properties beyond surface modifications, presenting new opportunities for enhancing their functionality in various technological applications [1,7,11].

This research undertakes a detailed DFT analysis to understand further the highly crystalline B-N Co-doped CDs previously produced through microwave-assisted synthesis [17]. This analysis elucidates their internal geometric structures. Unlike previous studies that primarily focus on surface properties, the present work examines the formation and arrangement of individual layers within the B-N Co-doped CDs. By treating each layer as an independent entity, a more comprehensive understanding of the overall structure of the carbon dots is achieved. This approach is supported by findings from Zaman et al. [15], who demonstrated that the molecular structure derived from biochar-based synthesis methods can significantly influence the detection capabilities of B-N Co-doped CDs, highlighting the interplay between internal structure and functional performance. Theoretical FTIR and Raman spectra are compared with experimental data to validate the models. This study investigates the ground-state geometries of B-N Co-doped CDs. It also aims to correlate theoretical spectra with experimental observations to enhance the understanding of the internal structural characteristics of these nanomaterials. These efforts aim to offer new insights into the complex architecture of doped B-N Co-doped CDs and lay the groundwork for future investigations into their structural and functional optimization.

This group previously reported the experimental synthesis of B-N Co-doped CDs in Ref. [17]. Briefly, a solution containing 1 mg/mL of 3-aminophenylboronic acid monohydrate was prepared by dissolving the compound in ultrapure water. The mixture was subsequently sonicated to ensure thorough dispersion, and the pH was adjusted to 5 using an acid. Following pH adjustment, the prepared solution was transferred to a microwave reactor and subjected to microwave irradiation for 3 min at 120 °C with a power setting of 150 W.

After microwave treatment, the resulting dispersion was purified to remove any unreacted precursors and by-products. Purification was carried out using methods such as centrifugation and dialysis, ensuring the isolation of high-purity B-N Co-doped CDs. The purified dispersion was then stored at 4 °C to maintain stability before characterization.

For characterization purposes, the synthesized B-N Co-doped CDs underwent Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and Raman spectroscopy analyses to confirm the successful co-doping of boron and nitrogen and to elucidate the structural properties of the B-N Co-doped CDs [17].

Fig. 1 shows the schematic representation of the model used to simulate the doped carbon dots (CDs). Experimental evidence suggests that the internal structure of the CD consists of multiple graphene layers, each exhibiting distinct doping and functionalization configurations. These layers incorporate heteroatoms, such as nitrogen and boron, as well as functional oxygen groups, which is consistent with experimental findings. However, current computing capabilities remain insufficient for such a calculation. The idealized model offers a helpful approximation of how the layers may be organized in the actual system, providing valuable insights into its possible structure.

Fig. 2 presents a set of representative layer configurations that incorporate the principal components identified through experimental analysis. These configurations were developed to compare calculated spectroscopic data and actual experimental results, utilizing information from Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). However, due to the significant size difference between theoretical models (approximately 100 atoms) and experimental systems (on the order of thousands of atoms), exact compositions could not be directly adjusted. Nevertheless, both systems were ensured to contain at least the same elemental components.

It is essential to note that the experimental system comprises flakes of varying sizes and compositions stacked together to form an approximately spherical structure, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1. The idealized flake model used in this study aims to represent one of the most characteristic structures in the field. Although interlayer interactions are not explicitly considered, this simplification is necessary. Considering these interactions would complicate the calculations significantly due to the large number of atoms and the extensive computational time required. The validity of this model is supported by experimental data as can be seen in Fig. 3 , which presents the XPS survey spectrum of the B-N Co-doped CDs, confirming the presence of boron, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, with atomic percentages of 7.15 %, 57.82 %, 6.81 %, and 28.22 %, respectively. Additionally, Table 1 lists the FTIR vibrational modes and XPS assignments, confirming the presence of boronic acid and amine functional groups, as well as nitrogen and boron defects within the material's carbon network. These findings align with the structural features considered in the model, reinforcing its relevance in representing the material’s composition and bonding environment.

The GAUSSIAN09 software [18] was utilized to perform density functional calculations to obtain ground-state geometries and determine B-N Co-doped CDs' structural, vibrational, and electronic properties. To solve the Kohn-Sham equations, the hybrid B3LYP [19–21] exchange–correlation functional was employed, along with the 6–31 g* basis set, which includes polarization functions [22] that enhance weak atomic interactions. This combination strikes a balance between computational costs and accuracy in describing interatomic interactions, thereby providing a better alignment with experimental measurements. This level of theory has been used to model the structural and electronic properties of organic molecules, as previously reported in the literature. [15,23–28].

For all calculations, fully unrestricted spin-polarized structural optimizations were performed to determine their equilibrium configurations and corresponding electronic and structural properties. The initial molecular structures were guided by results obtained from Raman measurements, and the optimized motifs were compared with experimental data from B–N Co-doped CDs. Despite inherent approximations, the results provide valuable insights for interpreting experimental observations.

The stability of the system was evaluated using the binding energy as a chemical descriptor, calculated as follows:
(1)
E b
( X )
= -
E
total
( X )
-
N 1
∗ E
atom
C
-
N 2
∗ E
atom
N
-
N 3
∗ E
atom
H
-
N 4
∗
E
atom
( B )
-
N 5
∗ E
atom
( O )
N t
where Eb represents the binding energy, Etotal(x) is the total energy of the B-N Co-doped CDs, and Ni (with i = 1 to 5) is the total number of carbon, nitrogen, hydrogen, boron, and oxygen atoms, respectively. Eatom is the energy of the isolated atoms, and Nt is the total number of atoms in the molecule. It is worth noting that the computed structures correspond to local minima on the complex potential energy surface, as indicated by the absence of imaginary vibrational frequencies in our optimization. Their positive binding energy values (see Eq. (1)) suggest thermodynamic favorability with respect to dissociation into isolated fragments. Although a global minimum search was not conducted, the presence of finite electronic gaps (≥0.70 eV) in all the computed structures indicates electronic and thermal stability. These results demonstrate that the identified configurations are locally stable and represent relevant candidates within the configurational space that may be accessible under specific synthesis or kinetic conditions [29]. Vibrational frequencies were calculated by diagonalizing the full Hessian matrix within the harmonic approximation [30]. The theoretical FTIR and Raman spectra were directly compared with experimental data. The resulting stick spectra were broadened with a Gaussian line shape function of 10 cm−1 to enhance the resolution of the spectra. A weighted average FTIR spectrum was calculated using the energies of the molecules as a reference. Since the energy differences between the molecules are very similar (as discussed in the results section), the normalization factor for the weighted average was determined, assuming all molecules have an equal probability of coexisting in the sample.

Studying vibrational properties in nanomaterials, such as B-N Co-doped CDs, is crucial for optimizing their functionalization and, consequently, their performance in advanced technological applications. Vibrational modes directly reflect the presence of structural defects and functional groups, providing a valuable tool for tuning the material's electronic and mechanical interactions. This section examines the influence of C-B and C-N bonds, as well as vacancy-type defects, on the material's final properties by analyzing vibrational modes observed through both theoretical and experimental approaches.

Fig. 2 presents the lowest energy atomic configurations of the B-N Co-doped CDs as proposed based on experimental data, along with their corresponding binding energies. These values range from 5.39 to 5.60 eV/atom, indicating very small differences between them. This narrow range suggests a high likelihood that these configurations could coexist during experimental measurements, contributing to the material's structural diversity and functional versatility. Although a binding energy window of 0.2 eV/atom may appear significant, variations in composition and structural motifs introduce uncertainty in determining relative energy levels and effective temperature differences among samples. Consequently, adopting a broader energy window is a more realistic approach to interpreting these systems.

Several key observations emerge from the structural analysis of the B-N Co-doped CDs. Firstly, the most probable structure present in the experiments features five molecules of boronic acid, B(OH)2, positioned at the edges of the graphene sheet. This edge functionalization is crucial for enhancing the solubility and dispersibility of the B-N Co-doped CDs, thereby improving their applicability in various technological applications.

Fig. 2(d) illustrates a graphene sheet with a vacancy, forming an octagonal carbon ring with an energy of 5.53 eV/atom. The presence of such vacancies introduces localized electronic states, significantly influencing the electronic and optical properties of the B-N Co-doped CDs. Notably, in Fig. 2(d), 2(e), 2(k), 2(l), and 2(m), the flakes are functionalized with oligomers that exhibit defects such as vacancies. These defects are pivotal in tuning B-N Co-doped CDs' electronic interactions and charge carrier dynamics.

Fig. 2(k) is particularly noteworthy because it features a defect involving a boron atom. Although two boron atoms are within the same ring (adjacent bond), the defect remains unstable, and the two boron atoms remain bonded. This finding suggests that adding boron can enhance certain properties but may also complicate the structural stability of B-N Co-doped CDs.

Fig. 2(l) and 2(i) stand out among the flakes with vacancies. Fig. 2(l) features two pairs of B-N bonds, whereas Fig. 2(i) has only one pair. This difference demonstrates how the arrangement of B-N bonds influences the structural and electronic properties of B-N Co-doped CDs. Additionally, configurations featuring pyrrole and structural arrangements with B-N bonds highlight the role of nitrogen in stabilizing the boron sites and contributing to the overall aromaticity and electronic properties of the B-N Co-doped CDs.

The coexistence of multiple low-energy configurations and intrinsic defects, such as vacancies and boron-related distortions, contributes to the structural diversity of the B-N Co-doped CDs.The presence of vacancies and boron-related defects plays a critical role in modulating the electronic structure of the B-N Co-doped CDs. These defects can act as active sites for catalysis, influence charge carrier mobility, and enhance interactions with functional groups, thereby improving the performance of B-N Co-doped CDs in applications such as bioimaging, sensing, and photovoltaics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical FTIR and Raman spectra of B-N Co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a) to 4(m) present DFT-calculated FTIR spectra for the 13 theoretical configurations of B-N Co-doped CDs. Each spectrum includes a color bar indicating the different vibration modes, as summarized in Table 1. The vibration modes are classified as light orange for boronic group vibrations, light blue for vibrations involving the amine group, and light green for C–C interaction vibrations. Fig. 4(n) displays the experimental FTIR spectrum of the B-N Co-doped CDs for comparison.

Analysis of the theoretical spectra reveals several important correlations with experimental observations. The N–H scissoring mode at 702 cm−1 is faintly represented in the spectra in Fig. 4(b), 4(e), and 4(k), indicating structures that accommodate accessible N–H bonds and support this vibration. The B-O–H deformation vibration at 1030 cm−1 is moderately intense in spectra in Fig. 4(a), 4(c), and 4(m), reflecting interactions involving boron and hydroxyl groups. C-B stretching, crucial at 1091 cm−1, is distinctly present in spectra in Fig. 4(d), 4(g), and 4(j), indicating strong bonding environments around boron atoms in these structures. Similarly, the B-O–H bending mode at 1201 cm−1 is prominent in spectra in Fig. 4(a) and 4(m), suggesting symmetrical environments around the boronic acid groups, essential for the functionalization of these B-N Co-doped CDs.

The C-N stretching mode at 1337 cm−1 is prominent in spectra in Fig. 4(f), 4(i), and 4(l), corresponding to well-defined C-N bonds, as seen experimentally. The B-O stretching mode at 1341 cm−1 is distinctly observed in spectra in Fig. 4(a) and 4(c), though absent in others, indicating that specific boron arrangements in the CD structures exhibit this vibration. The aromatic CC bending modes observed experimentally at 1441 and 1491 cm−1 are present across all spectra, with heightened intensity in Fig. 4(b), 4(f), and 4(k), emphasizing the aromatic character pervasive in these configurations. The asymmetric CC bending mode at 1580 cm−1 is observed in spectra in Fig. 4(b), 4(g), and 4(k), further underscoring the aromatic stability within these arrangements.

N–H bending at 1624 cm−1 is strongly represented in spectra in Fig. 4(e), 4(h), and 4(l), indicating environments with free N–H bonds capable of undergoing this vibration. However, the CN stretching mode at 2155 cm−1 is notably absent in all theoretical spectra. This suggests that CN groups are either underrepresented or absent in these models, potentially warranting a reevaluation of the structural assumptions. The N–H overtone at 2588 cm−1 barely appears in spectra in Fig. 4(d) and 4(g), consistent with limited overtone transitions, while C–H aromatic stretching at 2891 cm−1 is observed in spectra in Fig. 4(b), 4(h), and 4(m), reflecting subtle variations in hydrogen bonding and ring environments. The O–H stretching mode, critical at 3350 cm−1, is strongly present in spectra in Fig. 4(a), 4(e), and 4(m), confirming accessible hydroxyl groups in these structures. Finally, the N–H stretching modes at 3503 and 3524 cm−1 are prominent in spectra in Fig. 4(c), 4(f), and 4(k), correlating with structures where N–H groups are unhindered.

The experimental FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4(n)) captures most of the expected vibrational modes, though some deviations and band shifts are observed, which align more closely with specific theoretical structures. The CN stretching mode (∼2210–2260 cm−1), although present in trace amounts, shows a very weak signal in the experimental spectrum and plays a minimal role in the interaction with the graphene flake surface (perimeter); for these reasons, it was excluded from the theoretical model. This study focused on functional groups with significant chemical and spectroscopic relevance to the graphene perimeter, including –NH2, –BO2H2, –OH, –NO2H2, –CO, as well as nitrogen and boron dopants and vacancy defects. The detailed comparison between experimental and DFT-predicted spectra demonstrates good overall agreement, confirming the model's predictive power for most vibrational modes while highlighting the need for refinement or alternative configurations to account for molecular heterogeneity and residual discrepancies.

Experimental measurements on B-N Co-doped CDs, hypothesized to be spherical arrangements of carbonaceous sheets with a crystalline core and functionalized surroundings, prompted an investigation into the interatomic distances between bilayer structures. To gain insight into the physics and mechanisms involved in CD formation, a system consisting of two flakes was modeled: one with a nitrogen atom and the other with a boron atom, as shown in Fig. 5 (a) and 5(b). In this model, the distances between the disks were varied to evaluate the energy at each step and the force between the sheets. Recognizing the complexity of these systems, which far exceeds that of a simple carbon sheet with a single impurity, the model was extended to include two sheets with multiple components, as depicted in Fig. 5(c) and 5(d). Also, possible misalignment was considered by introducing a rotation between the sheets and calculating their equilibrium configuration.

In Fig. 5(e), the energy difference between the bilayer configuration and the separated flakes exhibits a minimum of approximately 20 meV/atom at the equilibrium distance. This result confirms the stability of the composite system and highlights the tendency for increased stability through layering. Although this energy per atom appears small compared to the thermal energy at room temperature, it remains significant, as the reference state consists of individually stable layers, and this value represents the additional stabilization induced by interlayer interactions.

The interlayer binding energy is expected to increase with the addition of more layers, leading to enhanced overall stability. Experimental results from CD studies have demonstrated that these structures remain stable at and above room temperature, further supporting this trend. A polynomial fit was applied to the energy data to quantify the interlayer forces, and its derivative was computed. As Fig. 5(f) illustrates, the equilibrium position, corresponding to the minimum potential energy (zero net force), occurs at approximately 3.7 Å. The results indicate variations in interlayer spacing within the carbon sheets, with greater disparity in the multicomponent system, as shown in Fig. 5(c) and 5(d), where the spacing ranges from 0.34 to 0.4 nm.

This enhanced stability is associated with reduced average interplanar distance, which may decrease to approximately 2.5 Å, as observed experimentally. Although computational constraints prevented extending the calculations to systems with more than two layers, the agreement between the model predictions and experimental trends suggests that the proposed approach effectively captures the general behavior of the system.

The hypothesis that the experimental FTIR spectrum of B–N Co-doped carbon dots (CDs) arises from a combination of various structural configurations was investigated by constructing theoretical spectral mixtures (Fig. 6 ). These mixtures include color-coded bars that indicate the vibrational modes listed in Table 1, as well as visual representations of selected vibrational motions. The first mixture (Fig. 6(a)), comprising the three most stable configurations (Fig. 2(a)–2(c)), successfully reproduces key vibrational features observed in the experimental spectrum. Notably, the N–H scissoring mode at 702 cm−1 and the aromatic CC bending modes at 1441 cm−1 and 1491 cm−1 are well captured and are consistent with prominent peaks in the experimental data. Moreover, the theoretical mixture retains the characteristic band near 2500 cm−1, primarily attributed to Fig. 2(b) and 2(i) configurations, in good agreement with experimental observations. The pronounced enhancement of peaks in the 3000–4000 cm−1 region, corresponding to O–H and N–H stretching modes, further supports the relevance of these structural motifs in representing the surface chemistry of the B–N co-doped CDs. These findings indicate that, despite representing a limited subset, the selected structural configurations—motivated by experimental observations—capture essential vibrational features of the measured FTIR spectrum. This supports their relevance as plausible molecular models and provides valuable insight into the structural characteristics of B–N Co-doped CDs.

The alignment with the experimental spectrum improved further when the mixture was expanded to include six structures (Fig. 6(b)). This six-structure mixture retained the essential vibrational modes identified in the three-structure combination while also increasing the complexity and intensity of the spectrum, particularly in the high-frequency region. The broader and more pronounced peaks in the 3000 to 4000 cm−1 range suggest a collective contribution from multiple O–H and N–H groups. This reinforces the hypothesis that the experimental B-N Co-doped CDs are heterogeneous systems with coexisting functional groups.

The mixture incorporating ten structures (Fig. 6(c)) offered a strong match to the experimental spectrum, accurately replicating its broad and continuous profile. The smoothing and broadening of peaks, especially in the aromatic CC bending and O–H/N–H stretching regions, suggest that the experimental spectrum results from the superposition of multiple vibrational modes across various structural configurations. However, the mixture of all thirteen structures (Fig. 6(d)) provides the most comprehensive representation of the experimental data. This full-structure mixture captures the previously identified features. It enhances the alignment in the lower wavenumber regions, such as the 750 cm−1 peak, which was less pronounced in the ten-structure mixture. The 13-structure mixture closely mirrors the experimental data, suggesting that the intricate vibrational characteristics of B-N Co-doped CDs can be accurately replicated only by considering the full diversity of possible configurations. As shown in the figure, some representative vibrational modes are displayed as insets, corresponding to specific bands in the experimental FTIR. For example, in the experimental spectrum, the band at 3350 cm−1 appears as a single peak, whereas in the theoretical results, this band is split into a double peak. This is due to the contribution of two O–H stretching modes of the boronic acid group (antisymmetric and symmetric).

These results highlight the necessity of considering multiple structural configurations when analyzing complex nanoscale materials such as B-N Co-doped CDs. The strong correlation between the thirteen-structure mixture and the experimental spectrum underscores the effectiveness of this approach, confirming that a superposition of spectra is crucial for accurately representing the intricate vibrational characteristics of these systems. This analysis substantiates the proposed structural hypotheses and highlights the effectiveness of integrating advanced DFT calculations with experimental observations to enhance the understanding of Co-doped carbon dots.

Fig. 7 presents the theoretical Raman spectra for the 13B-N Co-doped CD structures. These spectra strongly correlate with the experimental results reported in Ref. [17]. The experimental Raman spectrum is characterized by prominent D and G bands at approximately 1250 cm−1 and 1500 cm−1, respectively. These bands are attributed to the graphitic nature of the B-N Co-doped CDs and the incorporation of heteroatoms such as boron and nitrogen. The D band corresponds to induced structural defects, while the G band relates to the in-plane stretching of sp2 hybridized CC bonds within the carbon network.

Similar vibrational modes are observed in the theoretical spectra, confirming that the D and G bands are consistent features across different structural configurations of the B-N Co-doped CDs. However, slight shifts in peak positions compared to the experimental data suggest that the theoretical models, while effective in capturing the general vibrational characteristics, may not fully account for the complex environmental interactions present during actual synthesis. Factors such as temperature, pressure, and additional functional groups could contribute to the observed discrepancies, a hypothesis supported by the findings reported in Ref. [17].

The complex vibrational structures in some theoretical spectra, particularly those in Fig. 7(a), 7(g), and 7(k), align with the detailed analysis of defect-induced Raman features previously reported. These spectra likely reflect the contribution of localized defects, grain boundaries, and other structural irregularities noted in the experimental spectra, especially in samples subjected to varying synthesis conditions.

High-frequency modes between 3000 and 4000 cm−1, prominently observed in the theoretical spectrum of Fig. 7(e), correspond to overtone or combination bands potentially linked to N–H or O–H stretching vibrations. These modes are consistent with the presence of functional groups identified in the previous study [17], where they were recognized for their role in modulating the electronic and vibrational properties of B-N Co-doped CDs. However, the red-shift in the theoretical spectra relative to the experimental data suggests that environmental factors during synthesis, such as interactions with solvent molecules or other surface effects, might influence these high-frequency modes.

The theoretical Raman spectra provide valuable insights into the structural and functional aspects of B-N Co-doped CDs, validating their graphitic and defect-rich nature. The observed correlations and discrepancies between theoretical and experimental spectra highlight the importance of considering environmental and synthesis-related factors in modeling the vibrational properties of complex nanomaterials.

Fig. 8 presents the Raman spectra of various mixtures of B-N Co-doped CD structures alongside the experimental spectrum for direct comparison. The figure sequentially displays mixtures of the three most stable structures (Fig. 8a), followed by mixtures of six (Fig. 8b), ten (Fig. 8c), and all thirteen structures (Fig. 8d), with the experimental spectrum presented at the bottom (Fig. 8e). In general, alignment between the theoretical mixtures and the experimental spectrum is particularly noteworthy across the full range from 0 to 4000 cm−1. Theoretical spectra effectively capture key vibrational features, including the prominent peaks between 3000 and 4000 cm−1. These high-frequency peaks are likely associated with overtone or combination bands involving functional groups such as N–H and O–H. These modes are crucial as they provide insights into the surface chemistry and functionalization of the B-N Co-doped CDs, aligning closely with observations from previous experimental studies [17]. The presence of these high-frequency modes in both theoretical and experimental spectra suggests that the models effectively capture the functional group dynamics integral to CD’s photophysical properties.

Despite these similarities in the high-frequency region, some discrepancies emerge in the lower wavenumber regions, particularly around 750 cm−1. This peak, present in some individual theoretical spectra, becomes notably diminished or even absent in the mixture of ten structures (Fig. 8c). However, when all thirteen structures are included (Fig. 8d), this peak reappears more prominently, resembling the experimental spectrum. This observation suggests that the vibrational mode responsible for the 750 cm−1 peak is linked to specific configurations that may only be fully represented when all structures are considered. The attenuation of this feature in the ten-structure mixture implies that it may lack the necessary structural diversity to fully encapsulate the range of configurations present in the actual B-N Co-doped CDs. Thus, including a comprehensive set of structural models is crucial for accurately replicating the experimental spectrum. The re-emergence of this peak in the thirteen-structure mixture highlights the need to consider the full spectrum of possible configurations in theoretical models to capture the complexity and heterogeneity of the experimental material.

Furthermore, the consistent presence of the D (∼1350 cm−1) and G (∼1600 cm−1) bands across all mixtures suggests that the theoretical models accurately reflect the fundamental structural characteristics of the B-N Co-doped CDs. The D band is linked to defect-induced breathing modes, while the G band corresponds to the in-plane stretching of sp2 carbon atoms. Both bands are essential for understanding the graphitic nature and defect landscape of the B-N Co-doped CDs. Variations in the intensity of the D band among the different mixtures indicate differences in defect densities and types, reflecting the diversity of the CD structures. Moreover, the sharper G bands in the theoretical mixtures contrast with the broader G band observed in the experimental spectrum, which may indicate a higher degree of structural disorder or a more heterogeneous distribution of graphitic domains in the actual material. This difference further emphasizes the role of structural diversity in accurately capturing the experimental vibrational signature, as the broader experimental features suggest the presence of a wider array of structural configurations than those captured by the ten-structure mixture alone.

Fig. 9 depicts the region between 1000 and 2000 cm−1, encompassing the D and G bands, where the structural characteristics of the B-N Co-doped CDs are most prominently revealed. The figure features color bands that correspond directly to the colors of the lines in the deconvolution graph of the Raman spectra for this range. These color-coded bands visually emphasize the alignment between the deconvoluted spectral components and their corresponding vibrational modes, facilitating a more straightforward interpretation of the underlying structural features.

The D band, centered around ∼1350 cm−1, is typically associated with defect-induced breathing modes of sp2 carbon networks. Its intensity directly indicates the level of disorder within the material. In contrast, the G band, located at ∼1600 cm−1, corresponds to the in-plane stretching vibrations of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and is a hallmark of graphitic structures. The theoretical mixtures accurately reproduce these bands, with the three- and six-structure combinations showing close alignment with the experimental peak positions and intensities (Fig. 9a and 9b). This precise reproduction suggests that theoretical models effectively capture the fundamental vibrational characteristics of the graphitic core within the B-N Co-doped CDs. Furthermore, the broadening observed in the experimental D band, also evident in the mixed spectra, suggests a variety of defect types, including vacancies, edge defects, and heteroatom substitutions within the B-N Co-doped CDs, thereby validating the structural insights derived from theoretical models.

The experimental spectrum has been further deconvoluted into several distinct bands, each corresponding to specific vibrational modes that provide a deeper understanding of the structural configurations and dopant interactions within the B-N Co-doped CDs. For instance, the SL band (900–1075 cm−1) represents the fundamental breathing mode of smaller polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), such as benzene, and the secondary breathing modes of naphthalene. Its presence in the experimental data suggests the existence of smaller aromatic units within the B-N Co-doped CDs, potentially stabilized by boron and nitrogen dopants. However, the attenuation of this band in theoretical mixtures indicates that these smaller units might be underrepresented or that their contributions are being averaged.

The S-band (1150–1200 cm−1) corresponds to the frustrated breathing modes of larger PAH regions containing graphitic nitrogen, as well as the secondary breathing modes of non-hexagonal rings involving heteroatoms. This band is strongly represented in the experimental spectrum, reflecting significant nitrogen incorporation and its corresponding structural effects, which are accurately captured in the theoretical spectra.

The DS band (1250–1300 cm−1) is associated with the fundamental breathing modes of small PAHs, such as pyrene, and the breathing of pyrene-like moieties within larger PAHs. This highlights the critical role of defect-related vibrations within the B-N Co-doped CDs. The D band (1340–1380 cm−1) is the most significant feature in this region, representing the fundamental vibrational frequency for a wide range of PAHs with various defects, grain size variations, and heteroatom incorporations. The A1 band (1400–1460 cm−1) relates to the A1g fundamental breathing mode of smaller PAHs like naphthalene and pyrene, which are shifted due to defects and heteroatoms. This band indicates complex structural configurations within the B-N Co-doped CDs. The A2 band (1480–1550 cm−1) includes vibrations related to heteroatoms and sp3 carbon character within the B-N Co-doped CDs. Lastly, the C band (1750–1800 cm−1) represents carbonyl stretching modes, indicating oxygen-containing functional groups on the CD surface. These bands collectively highlight the structural complexity and heterogeneity of the B-N Co-doped CDs, with theoretical models capturing many, but not all, of the experimentally observed vibrational characteristics.

This work provides a comprehensive DFT-based investigation of B–N Co-doped carbon dots (CDs), focusing on their vibrational behavior and structural characteristics. Analysis of thirteen distinct configurations reveals a consistent match between calculated and experimental FTIR and Raman spectra, supporting the presence of layered structures in experimental samples. The study reveals that weak interlayer interactions yield spectral features that are most accurately reproduced by combining the spectra of multiple structures. This spectral superposition approach demonstrates that structural diversity—not just the number of contributing configurations—is key to achieving agreement with experimental data.

The findings indicate that specific bilayer motifs predominantly influence the vibrational signatures, offering valuable insights into the probable atomic arrangements within experimental samples. The results highlight the capacity of DFT to capture essential vibrational characteristics of doped CDs and provide a predictive framework for structural analysis. Although the number of configurations analyzed is limited due to computational cost, this methodology establishes a scalable approach for bridging theory and experiment. Future work should expand the configurational space and incorporate environmental effects, such as the solvent and temperature effects, to improve the accuracy of the models. This study provides a foundation for the rational design of doped CDs and opens avenues for targeted bioimaging, sensing, and energy conversion applications.

D.C. Navarro-Ibarra: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. H.D. Ibarra-Prieto: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. A. Garcia-Garcia: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. F. Aguilera-Granja: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the computer resources from the IPICYT’S National Supercomputer Center by the computational time grant TKII-E-0424-I-090424-20/PR-22, the CONAHCyT Ph.D. fellowship #744216 and CIMAV grant number 25003 (internal project).
